title: EXPOSURE XVI THEOREM OF BASE CHANGE BY A SMOOTH MORPHISM, AND APPLICATIONS

pages.title: EXPOSURE XVI THEOREM OF BASE CHANGE BY A SMOOTH MORPHISM, AND APPLICATIONS

blocks.html:
EXPOSEMENT XVI
THEOREM OF BASE CHANGE BY A SMOOTH MORPHISM, AND APPLICATIONSby M. Artin
Summary

1. The base change theorem by a smooth morphism.
2. The specialization theorem of cohomology groups.
3. The relative cohomological purity theorem.
4. The cohomology comparison theorem for algebraic preschemes on \(\mathbb{C}\).
5. The finiteness theorem for algebraic preschemas with characteristic zero.

pages.title: 1. The base change theorem by a smooth morphism.

blocks.html: 1. The base change theorem by a smooth morphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

Let \(\mathbb{L} \subset \mathbb{P}\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and

\[(*)\qquad \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} & X' \ar[l]_{g'} \ar[d]^{f'} \\ Y & Y' \ar[l]_{g} }\]

a Cartesian diagram, with \(f\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated, and \(g\) universally locally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. univ. loc. \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\), resp. univ. loc. \(n\)-acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\)) (XV 1.11). Then for each sheaf \(F\) of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. abelian of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) on \(X\), the base change morphism (XII 4.1.2)

\[\varphi^q : g^*(R^q f_*)F \longrightarrow (R^q f'_*)g'^* F\]

is bijective for \(q = 0\) (resp. \(q \leqslant 1\), resp. \(q \leqslant n\)).
In particular, by applying XV 2.1, we find the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2

(Theorem of base change by a smooth morphism). Suppose that the morphism \(g\) of (*) is smooth, that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separate, and that \(\mathbb{L} \subset \mathbb{P}\) is the complementary set to the set of residual characteristics of \(Y\). Then for each sheaf \(F\) of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. abelian of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) on \(X\), the base change morphism \(\varphi^q\) above is bijective for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\), resp. for each \(q\)).

Demonstration of 1.1. Let us first deal with the case where \(f : X \to Y\) is quasi-projective: it is only a conjunction of the base change theorem for a proper morphism XII 5.1 and the definition XV 1.9. Indeed, we have a commutative diagram

where \(\overline{f}\) is projective, therefore proper, and where \(i\) is an open immersion. Now we know XII 5.1 that the base change theorem is true for \(\overline{f}\) whatever \(g : Y' \to Y\). Since \(g \times_Y \overline{Y} : Y' \times_Y \overline{Y} \to \overline{Y}\) is still universally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. ...), we are immediately reduced by (XII 4.4 (ii)) to proving the theorem for the morphism \(i\), that is to say, we are brought back to the case where \(f\) is an open immersion. Then the theorem is a consequence of definition XV 1.10 (ii).
Let us now deal with the general case. The assertion is local to \(Y\), and we can therefore assume \(Y\) affine.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3

We can also assume \(X\) affine.

Demonstration. Proceeding as in XII 6.1, to prove 1.1 for a given \(f\) we are reduced to proving it in the situation deduced from the given situation by changes of base \(\widetilde{Y} \to Y\), \(\widetilde{Y}' \to Y'\), for a local morphism \(\widetilde{g} : \widetilde{Y}' \to \widetilde{Y}\) of strict localities of \(Y',Y\) induced by \(g\), and to prove that homomorphisms

are bijective for \(q \leqslant n\), where \(\widetilde{X} = X \times_Y \widetilde{Y}\), \(\widetilde{X}' = X' \times_{Y'} \widetilde{Y}'\). This therefore brings us back to the case where \(Y,Y',g\) are strictly local, and to proving in this case the bijectivity of the previous applications, admitting that 1.1 is proven for \(f\) affine. Now let \((X_i)\) be a finite covering of \(X\) by open affines \(X_i\), and let \(Z\) be the sum schema of \(X_i\), which is therefore affine and equipped with a surjective morphism \(h : Z \to X\), hence a morphism \(h' : Z' \to X'\). Note that \(h\) is separated, and that it is affine when \(f\) is separated. That said, applying the descent lemma XII 6.8, we see that to prove the bijectivity of the maps (*) for \(q \leqslant n\) and for all \(F\), it suffices to prove the bijectivity of the corresponding maps \(H^q(Z,h^*(F)) \to H^q(Z',h'^*(F'))\), provided that the base change homomorphisms, for \(Z \to X\) and the base change \(X' \to X\), are isomorphisms in dimension \(\leqslant n\). Now if \(f\) is separated, therefore \(h\) is affine, it is thus by hypothesis, which proves 1.1 when \(f\) is assumed to be separated. In the general case, we can then apply the previous result to \(h\) which is still separated, and still conclude that 1.1 is true for \(f\), which proves 1.3.

Note. We could also invoke Leray's spectral sequence for the open covering \((X_i)\) of \(X\), and its non-commutative variants, but this method, which is not essentially different from recourse to XII 6.8, has the disadvantage of forcing us to distinguish again the three usual cases, work which has already been done in loc.cit.

To complete the proof of the theorem in the general case, it suffices to bring the proof back to the case where \(f : X \to Y\) is affine and of finite type, therefore quasi-projective. According to 1.3, we can assume \(X\) affine. Let then be \(X = \varprojlim X_\alpha\), where \(f_\alpha : X_\alpha \to Y\) are affine schemes of finite type on \(Y\). We finish with the technique of passing the usual limit (VII 5).
Add-ons 1.4.

a) We leave it to the reader to note that the same demonstration still gives a conclusion when we assume that \(g\) is universally locally \((-1)\)-acyclic (XV 1.14): in this case, the base change homomorphism

is injective for any sheaf of sets \(F\) over \(X\). On the other hand, suppose again \(n \geqslant 0\), we can complete 1.1 with the following injectivity statement, also contained in the preceding proof: if \(g\) is universally locally \(0\)-acyclic, then for any sheaf in groups \(F\) on \(X\), the homomorphism of base change \(\varphi^1\) of 1.1 is a monomorphism; if \(g\) is universally locally \(n\)-acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\), then the homomorphism \(\varphi^{n+1}\) of 1.1 is a monomorphism for every abelian sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion \(F\) on \(X\).

b) It is undoubtedly possible to also give an analogous injectivity conclusion from the hypothesis of \(1\)-local asphericity for \(\mathbb{L}\) of \(g\), by introducing the invariants of \(2\)-non-commutative cohomology of GIRAUD's thesis, compare XII 5.11 (∗); the same question also arises for (1.6) and (2.3) below.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.5

Let \(K/k\) be a body extension, and let \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P} - \{p\}\), where \(p = \operatorname{car} k\). Then \(\operatorname{Spec}(K) \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is universally locally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally locally \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\); if \(k\) and \(K\) are separably closed, then the preceding morphism is also universally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\).
Let us immediately note the following consequence of (1.5):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.6

Let \(K/k\) be an extension of separably closed bodies, \(X\) a schema, \(F\) a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. of abelian groups of \(\mathbb{L}\)-twist) to \(X\), where \(\mathbb{L} = \mathbb{P} - \{p\}\), \(p = \operatorname{car} k\). So the application

\[H^q(X,F) \longrightarrow H^q(X_K,F_K)\]

is bijective for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q \leqslant 1\), resp. for all \(q\)).

This in fact follows immediately from the fact that \(\operatorname{Spec}(K) \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is universally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\), and from the definitions.— Note that we can also interpret the previous homomorphism on the \(H^q\) as being identified, by VIII 2.4 to the base change homomorphism

where \(f : X \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(f' : X_K \to \operatorname{Spec}(K)\), \(g = \operatorname{Spec}(K) \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) and \(g' : X_K \to X\) are the obvious morphisms. So when \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the bijectivity of these homomorphisms can also be considered, thanks to 1.1, as a consequence of the fact that \(\operatorname{Spec}(K) \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is universally locally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally locally \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\). This last fact therefore already implies 1.6 for \(X\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate, which is clearly sufficient to entail that \(\operatorname{Spec}(K) \to \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) is universally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally \(1\)-aspheric for \(\mathbb{L}\) (XV 1.7, 1.6 (i)).

This therefore shows that to prove 1.5, it is enough to prove the first assertion of 1.5. Even if it means moving to the perfect closure of \(k\), which is lawful by VIII 1.1, we can then assume \(k\) perfect. Any extension \(K\) of \(k\) is an inductive limit of its subalgebras \(A\) of finite type on \(k\), and \(k\) being perfect, even if it means locating such a \(A\), we can assume it to be smooth, so that \(K\) appears as a filtering inductive limit of smooth subalgebras. The latter being universally locally acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\) and universally locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\) by virtue of XV 2.1, we conclude thanks to XV 1.13 (ii), c.q.f.d.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.7

We will compare the invariance theorem 1.5 to XII 5.4, where we did not have to assume the torsion beam considered first with the residual characteristics, but where on the other hand we must assume \(X\) proper on \(k\). Already in the case of \(H^1(X,\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})\), where \(X\) is an affine algebraic curve (for example the affine line) on an algebraically closed field \(k\) of car. \(p > 0\), the analogy of the previous statements becomes false, because of the phenomena of "immoderate ramification" to infinity, implying that in such a case the classification of the main flat coverings of group \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\) is essentially "continuous".

pages.title: 2. Theorem of specialization of cohomology groups.

blocks.html: 2. Specialization theorem of cohomology groups.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let \(\mathbb{L} \subset \mathbb{P}\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a proper finite presentation morphism (∗) and locally \(0\)-acyclic (resp. loc. \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), resp. loc. \(n\)-acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\)). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. abelian of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) constructible and locally constant on \(X\). Then the \(R^q f_* F\) are constructible and locally constant for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\), resp. for \(q \leqslant n\)) and for any geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\), we have

\[(R^q f_* F)_{\bar{y}} \simeq H^q(X_{\bar{y}},F_{\bar{y}}) \;,\]

for these same values of \(q\).
Note. Note that the last assertion is only the base change theorem for a proper morphism XII 5.2.
Given XV 2.1, we immediately have the following corollary:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

(Specialization theorem for cohomology groups). Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a clean and smooth morphism, and \(\mathbb{L} \subset \mathbb{P}\) the complementary set to the set of residual characteristics of \(Y\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. abelian of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) constructible on \(X\). Then the \(R^q f_*(F)\) are constructible and locally constant for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\), resp. for all \(q\)), and for any geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\), we have

\[R^q f_*(F)_{\bar{y}} \simeq H^q(X_{\bar{y}}, F_{\bar{y}})\]

for these same values of \(q\).
Note. Taking \(n = 1\), and translating in terms of fundamental groups, we find (SGA1 X 3.8) comparing the fundamental groups of fibers of a clean and smooth morphism.

Demonstration of 2.1. Since we already know that the \(R^q f_*(F)\) are constructible XIV 1.1, thanks to IX 2.11 it suffices to prove that for any specialization morphism \(\bar{y}_1 \to \bar{y}_0\) of geometric points of \(Y\), the corresponding specialization morphism (VIII 7.7)

is bijective for the values ​​of \(q\) considered. On this subject we have the following slightly more general result (where it is useless to assume \(f\) of finite presentation and \(F\) constructible):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(\mathbb{L} \subset \mathbb{P}\), \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), \(f : X \to Y\) be a clean and locally \(0\)-acyclic morphism (resp. locally \(1\)-aspherical for \(\mathbb{L}\), resp. locally \(n\)-acyclic for \(\mathbb{L}\)), \(F\) a sheaf of sets (resp. of ind-\(\mathbb{L}\)-groups, resp. abelian of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion) on \(X\), which is locally constant. Then for any specialization morphism \(\bar{y}_1 \to \bar{y}_0\) of geometric points of \(Y\), the corresponding specialization homomorphism \((*)\) above is bijective for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\), resp. for \(q \leqslant n\)). Moreover, in the case \(n = 0\) i.e. \(g\) locally \(0\)-acyclic, for any sheaf of locally constant groups \(F\) over \(X\), the homomorphism \(R^1 f_*(F)_{\bar{y}_0} \to R^1 f_*(F)_{\bar{y}_1}\) is injective; if \(n\) is arbitrary, for any abelian sheaf of \(\mathbb{L}\)-torsion \(F\) locally constant on \(X\), the specialization homomorphism \((*)\) is injective for \(q = n+1\).

Under the conditions of 2.1, suppose \(Y\) connected, and let \(q \leqslant n\). Then the \(H^q(X_{\bar{y}}, F)\), for the geometric points \(y\) of \(Y\), are all isomorphic with each other.

blocks.html: 3. The relative cohomological purity theorem.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let \(S\) be a schema. We call \(S\)-smooth couple \((Y,X)\) a \(S\)-closed immersion \(i : Y \to X\) of \(S\)-smooth preschemes, that is to say, a commutative diagram of preschemes

\[\xymatrix{ Y \ar[rr]^{i} \ar[dr]_{h} & & X \ar[dl]^{f} \\ & S & }\]

such that \(f\) and \(h\) are smooth and \(i\) is a closed immersion. Note \(U = X - Y\), \(j : U \to X\) open immersion, and \(g : U \to S\) structural morphism. We call codimension of \((Y,X)\) at a point \(y \in Y\) the codimension in \(y\) of the fiber \(Y_s\) in \(X_s\), where \(s = h(y)\).
The codimension is therefore a locally constant function on \(Y\). We will indicate by the sentence "\((Y,X)\) is of codimension \(c\)" that this function is even constant, at value \(c\).
Let \((Y,X)\) be a \(S\)-smooth couple and \(y \in Y\). Recall (SGA II 4.10) that there exist integers \(m\) and \(n\), a neighborhood \(X'\) of \(y\) in \(X\), and an equal morphism
such that \(Y' = Y \cap X'\) is the inverse image of the closed subschema \(\mathbb{E}^m_S\) defined by the equations

We can express this result by saying that locally for the sparse topology, each \(S\)-smooth couple \((Y,X)\) is isomorphic to the standard couple \((\mathbb{E}^m_S, \mathbb{E}^n_S)\) for suitable \(m\) and \(n\), where

In this issue, we propose to calculate the local cohomology sheaves \(\underline{H}^q_Y(X,F) = (R^q i^!)F\) (V 4 and VIII 6.6) for a \(S\)-smooth couple \((Y,X)\), with values ​​in a locally constant abelian torsion beam \(F\) prime with residual characteristics. We start with preliminary considerations:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

Either

\[\xymatrix{ U \ar[rr]^{j} \ar[dr]_{g} & & X \ar[dl]^{f} \\ & S & }\]

a commutative diagram, where \(f\) is smooth, and \(j\) is an open immersion such that the fiber \(U_s\) is dense in \(X_s\) for each \(s \in S\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf of sets over \(S\). So the canonical morphism

\[f^*F \longrightarrow j_* g^* F = j_* j^* f^* F\]

Demonstration. We easily reduce to the case where \(U\) is retrocompact in \(X\). Let \(\bar{s}\) be a geometric point of \(S\), \(\bar{x}\) a geometric point of \(X\) above \(S\), \(\widetilde{f} : \widetilde{X} \to \widetilde{S}\) the morphism of the strict localities of \(S\), \(X\) at corresponding geometric points, and \(\widetilde{U} = U \times_X \widetilde{X}\). Therefore it suffices to demonstrate that \(\widetilde{g} : \widetilde{U} \to \widetilde{S}\) is \(0\)-acyclic, which will imply

Since \(\widetilde{g}\) is limit of smooth morphisms, it is locally \(0\)-acyclic (XV 2.1, 1.11), and by XV 1.12 it suffices to demonstrate that the geometric fibers of \(\widetilde{U}/\widetilde{S}\) are \(0\)-acyclic, that is to say connected and non-empty. Either \(\bar{s}'\) a geometric point of \(\widetilde{S}\). Alors la fibre \(\widetilde{X}_{\bar{s}'}\) is regular, connected and non-empty, and it results from the hypothesis that \(\widetilde{U}_{\bar{s}'}\) is a dense open of \(\widetilde{X}_{\bar{s}'}\), therefore connected and non-empty, hence the result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2.1

Under the conditions of 3.2, the functor \(X' \mapsto X'|U\) of the sparse coverings category of \(X\) in the slack coverings category of \(U\) is fully faithful. In particular, for any finite group \(G\), the restriction application

\[H^1(X,G) \longrightarrow H^1(U,G)\]

is injective.
This results from 3.2, via a standard argument which we will, for the convenience of references, explain in one

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.2.2

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a schema morphism, and let \(f^* : \operatorname{Et}(Y) \to \operatorname{Et}(X)\) be the inverse image functor \(Y' \mapsto Y' \times_Y X\) of the category \(\operatorname{Et}(Y)\) of the flat coverings of \(Y\) in \(\operatorname{Et}(X)\). Equivalent conditions:

(i) \(f^*\) is faithful (resp. fully faithful).

(ii) For any flat covering \(Y'\) of \(Y\), designating by \(f' : X' \to Y'\) the morphism deduced from \(f\) by change of base \(Y' \to Y\), the application \(U \mapsto f'^{-1}(U)\) of the set of both open and closed parts of the \(Y'\) in both open and closed parts of \(X'\) is injective (resp. bijective).

(ii bis) With the notations of (ii), for any constant sheaf of sets \(C\) on \(Y'\), the canonical map

\[H^0(Y',C) \longrightarrow H^0(X',f'^*C)\]

is injective (resp. bijective).

(iii) With the notations of (ii), the canonical application

\[\Gamma(Y'/Y) \longrightarrow \Gamma(X'/X)\]

is injective (resp. bijective).
The demonstration is left to the reader (see SGA 2, IX 3.1 and 3.2).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 3.2.3

We can considerably generalize 3.2, both in terms of bundles of sets, by weakening the hypotheses on \(f\), and in terms of bundles of groups with conclusions on the \(R^q g_*(f^*F)\) (cf. SGA 2 XIV and SGA 1 XIII). Similar note for 3.4 to 3.6.
Let us also "recall" the Zariski-Nagata purity theorem in its relative form:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.3

(Relative purity theorem). Either

\[\xymatrix{ U \ar[rr]^{j} \ar[dr]_{g} & & X \ar[dl]^{f} \\ & S & }\]

a commutative diagram where \(f\) is smooth, and where \(j\) is an open immersion such that for all \(s \in S\), \(X_s - U_s\) is everywhere of codimension \(\geqslant 2\) in \(X_s\). Then the functor \(X' \mapsto X'|U\) of the category of sparse coverings of \(X\) in the category of sparse coverings of \(U\) is an equivalence of categories.

Demonstration. That the functor is fully faithful has been seen (3.2.1). The fact that it is ess. surjective will be deduced from the classic theorem. We give ourselves a flat covering \(U'\) of \(U\), we must show that it extends into a flat covering \(X'\) of \(X\). Easy immediate reduction in case \(U\) is backward compact in \(X\), then in case \(S\) is affine and of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), \(X\) a connected scheme, and \(f\) of finite type.

We proceed by induction on \(n = \dim S\), the assertion being a consequence of the classic theorem (SGA 2 X 3.4) in the case \(n = 0\). Having uniqueness, we can apply the descent technique of SGA IX 4.7 to the normalized \(\overline{S} \to S\) to reduce to the case \(S\) (and therefore \(X\)) normal (\(\overline{S} \to S\) being finite (EGA IV 7.8.3 (iii) (vi))). Moreover, even if it means enlarging \(U\), we can assume \(U\) maximum among the open spaces above which \(U'\) can extend, and we must then prove \(U = X\). Otherwise, let \(x\) be a maximum point of \(X-U\), and \(s = f(x)\). It all comes down to proving that we can extend \(U'\) above a neighborhood of \(x\). We can assume that \(\dim \mathcal{O}_{S,s} = n \geqslant 1\) and that the theorem is already proven for the dimension \(< n\). Let \(B = \mathcal{O}_{X,x}\), and let \(B'\) be the normalized of \(B\) in the ring of rational functions on the covering \(U'\) of \(U\); therefore \(B'\) is a finite \(B\)-algebra (loc.cit.). It is obviously sufficient to demonstrate that \(B'\) is an \(B\)-even algebra.

Let \(A = \mathcal{O}_{S,s}\), \(0 \neq t \in \mathfrak{m}_A\), \(A_0 = A/tA\), \(B_0 = B/tB\). By induction hypothesis, the restriction of the covering to \(U_0 = U \otimes_A A_0\) extends to a flat covering of \(\operatorname{Spec} B_0\), let's call it \(\operatorname{Spec} B_0^*\). Even if it means replacing \(X\) by a suitable flat covering in the vicinity of \(x\) (which is permissible) we can assume \(\operatorname{Spec} B_0^*\) completely decomposed on \(\operatorname{Spec} B_0\). We are thus brought back to the case where the \(B_0\)-algebra \(B_0'\) is completely decomposed outside the closed point of \(\operatorname{Spec} B_0\). Then the assumptions of XV 3.3 are satisfied, hence the result.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Same assumptions as in 3.3. So we have

\[(R^1 j_*)g^* F = 0\]

for each sheaf \(F\) of ind-finite groups on \(S\).

Reduction as usual to the \(S\) Noetherian case, \(f\) of finite type, and \(F\) constructible. Let \(0 \to F \to G\) be an injection of ind-finite group sheaves on \(S\), and \(C = G/F\). It follows from 3.2 that the functor \(j_* g^*\) is exact, therefore that \(j_* g^* G \to j_* g^* C\) is surjective, therefore (XII 3.1) that \((R^1 j_*)g^* F \to (R^1 j_*)g^* G\) is injective. We can therefore replace \(F\) by \(G\), and we thus return to IX 2.14 and VIII 5.5 in the case where \(F = G\) is constant, given the value of an ordinary finite group \(G\). Remarks 3.10

a) We can in 3.8 explain the structure of the sheaf \(T_{Y/X}\), we find that it is canonically isomorphic to the sheaf \((\mu_n)_Y^{\otimes c}\), where \(\mu_n\) designates the sheaf of the \(n\)-th roots of the unit (locally isomorphic to the constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)). The isomorphism in question is studied under the name of local fundamental class of \(Y\) in \(X\), in the somewhat more general situation of relative complete intersections, in Exp. XVIII dedicated to duality.

b) It is very plausible that the conclusions of 3.9 and 3.10 a) remain valid without postulating the existence of a basic body \(k\), at least when \(X\) is an "excellent" preschema. This is what will be established in any case in Exp. XIX when \(X\) has zero characteristic, using the resolution of Hironaka singularities. As we will see in SGA 5 we need the "absolute purity theorem" (as well as the resolution of singularities) in particular to be able to establish the "local duality formula", (which itself is one of the major ingredients of the LEFSCHETZ-VERDIER formula).

pages.title: 5. The finiteness theorem for algebraic preschemas with characteristic zero.

blocks.html: 5. The finiteness theorem for algebraic preschemes with characteristic zero.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(k\) be a body of characteristic zero, and \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of finite type of schemas locally of finite type on \(k\). Let \(F\) be a constructible sheaf of sets (resp. finite groups, resp. abelian torsion) on \(X\). Then the \(R^q f_* F\) are also constructible for \(q = 0\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\), resp. for all \(q\)).

We assume that \(k\) has characteristic 0 because we will use the singularity resolution theorem [1]. The demonstration also applies to the characteristic \(p > 0\) if we admit the resolution of the singularities, provided that \(F\) is of prime orders at \(p\). We can therefore deduce a characteristic result \(\neq 0\) for \(\dim X \leqslant 2\), by applying the results of Abhyankar [3]. Another ingredient of the demonstration, in addition to the eternal th. of change of base for a proper morphism, is the "absolute cohomological purity theorem" 3.9.

Recall that the theorem is already proven for a proper morphism and for \(S\) arbitrary (XIV 1.1). Moreover, we will obtain in Exposé XIX (again in char. 0) the finiteness theorem under hypotheses much weaker than \(S\) is an excellent diagram and that \(f\) or of finite type, by proving for such schemes the absolute purity theorem in the form indicated in 3.10.
In the case of characteristic \(\neq 0\) and a dimension scheme \(\geqslant 3\), we currently only have the following easy consequence of XI 3.3:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2

Let \(X\) be a smooth algebraic scheme on \(\operatorname{Spec} k\), \(k\) a separably closed field, and let \(F\) be a sheaf of finite abelian groups (resp. finite groups) which is locally constant, and of prime orders with the characteristic of \(k\). Then \(H^q(X,F)\) is a finite group (resp. a finite pointed set) for all \(q\) (resp. for \(q = 0,1\)).

Demonstration of 5.2. We can assume \(k\) algebraically closed (VIII 1.1). Recursion on \(n = \dim X\). According to XI 3.3 there exists a hyper-covering of \(X\) by open spaces which are "good neighborhoods". We immediately reduce, by the spectral sequence of a hyper-covering, to the case where \(X\) is a good neighborhood, therefore admits an elementary fibration (XI 3.1)

According to (3.2, 3.6, 3.7), the beam \(j_* F\) is locally constant on \(\overline{X}\), \((R^1 j_*)F\) is locally constant on \(Y = \overline{X}-X\), and \((R^q j_*)F = 0\) if \(q > 0\). Consequently it follows from 2.1 that \((R^p \overline{f}_*)(R^q j_*)F\) is a locally constant sheaf on \(S\) for the values ​​of \(q\) considered, and the result results from the recurrence hypothesis and the Leray spectral sequence (resp. the exact sequence XII 3.2).

Demonstration of 5.1. The demonstration of 5.1 is very close to that of 4.1 for the constructible case \(F\). We will limit ourselves to indicating the main points: Recurrence on \(\dim X = n\). We first reduce ourselves to the case \(X\) and \(S\) affines, therefore \(f\) quasi-projective, by applying the method of the demonstration of 1.3. Then we apply IX 2.15 and (VIII 5.5 and 5.8) to reduce to the constant case \(F\). We have a commutative diagram

where \(h\) is a dense open immersion and where \(\overline{X}\) is clean, so the theorem is true for \(\overline{X}\) (XIV 1.1). It is thus sufficient to prove the theorem for \((h,F)\), that is to say for a dense open immersion and a constant beam. Let \(h : S' \to S\) be a resolution of the singularities of \(S\) and

the Cartesian diagram deduced from \(h\). We come back to proving the theorem for \(f'\), therefore in case \(S = S'\) is regular. By the recurrence method used at the end of the proof of 4.1, we reduce ourselves to the case where \(Y = S-X\) is non-singular, i.e. where \((Y,S)\) is a smooth couple, and we finish by applying (3.2, 3.4, 3.6, 3.7).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.3

The demonstration of 5.1 that we have just sketched is also valid when we give ourselves a Noetherian ring \(A\) on the left, canceled by an integer \(n > 0\) (which must be assumed to be prime in the characteristic if the latter is not assumed to be zero, cf. remarks following 5.1), and when we consider sheaves of \(A\)-buildable left modules.
